place: South Dennis CDP, New Jersey, New Jersey

facts.text: South Dennis once boasted a covered bridge spanning Dennis Creek, a historic landmark that linked the community to its early maritime roots.
The town’s gristmill shut its doors in the early 1890s, the same era the Sea Coast Railroad Company built a line through North and South Dennisville.
By the late 1920s, the landing and its wharves had largely disappeared, leaving only a line of abandoned piers for local fishermen.
Dennis Township remained a dry town until 2001, when voters approved a referendum that allowed liquor sales and earned the township $1.1 million.
The township was officially incorporated on March 1, 1827, carved out of Upper Township by an act of the New Jersey Legislature.
South Dennis is part of the South Jersey region and the larger Philadelphia‑Wilmington‑Camden combined statistical area, linking it to the Delaware Valley.
